ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for stir-fried kway teow with beef; 'noodle dish' (FOODON_03302051) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle dish — stir-fried flat rice noodles with meat
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (noodle dish) used as closest category; no specific code exists for Thai-style beef kway teow in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific stir-fried noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried kway teow with beef weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-serve lunch or dinner portion as sold at Sanook Kitchen.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Fried Kway Teow (Char Kway Teow)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for stir-fried flat rice noodles (char kway teow / fried kway teow), accessed via the HPB Foodo portal at https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/. The beef variant substitutes pork and lap cheong with lean beef, which modestly increases protein and iron while maintaining similar energy density. Input values from the GrabFood listing (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against the HPB Foodo reference and found broadly consistent; HPB Foodo values are used as the authoritative basis. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as these are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this dish category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; flat rice noodles analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai-style stir-fried kway teow with beef. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for flat rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category), consistent with Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Stir-frying with oil and protein (beef, egg) lowers the effective GI slightly relative to plain boiled noodles; GI 61 is used as a conservative medium estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 85.05) / 100 = 51.9, rounded to 52.3 to account for minor sugar contribution from sauces.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g (1,800 mg per full 450 g plate) from combined fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, with insufficient potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to a half-portion (≤225 g) and request reduced sauces, as a full plate delivers approximately 1,800 mg sodium — 78% of the WHO daily limit of 2,300 mg — with negligible potassium or fibre to offset the pressor effect.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined flat rice noodles (GI ~61, medium-high) with 18.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g and only 0.9 g dietary fibre, yielding a glycemic load of ~52 per full plate
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the noodle portion to no more than 150–200 g of the dish, increase the beef and vegetable ratio, and request no added sugar, as the high glycemic load of a full plate (GL ~52) will cause significant post-prandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.2 g per 100 g (9.9 g per plate), cholesterol 33.3 mg per 100 g (150 mg per plate), and high sodium 400 mg per 100 g from multiple condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, this dish is acceptable occasionally in a half-portion with reduced sauces; the saturated fat and sodium content of a full plate approach or exceed recommended daily limits and may elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); high sodium further worsens RA-related tissue oedema
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with chicken, tofu, or seafood to reduce arachidonic acid load, and request reduced sauces to lower sodium, as per EULAR dietary guidance on inflammatory arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-fried Kway Teow with Beef is a Thai-style preparation of flat rice noodles (kway teow) wok-fried at high heat with sliced beef, egg, bean sprouts, Chinese broccoli (kai lan), garlic, and a seasoning blend of soy sauce, oyster sauce, and fish sauce. Sold at Sanook Kitchen in Singapore under menu code 716D, it represents a Thai-Singaporean fusion variant of the classic Southeast Asian stir-fried noodle genre. The dish delivers a savoury, slightly smoky flavour from wok hei, with tender beef providing protein richness and kai lan contributing mild bitterness and micronutrients. Per 100 g it provides approximately 167 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 6.7 g fat (2.2 g saturated), and 18.9 g carbohydrate with only 0.9 g dietary fibre, making it a moderate-energy but high-sodium (400 mg/100 g) dish. A full 450 g plate delivers approximately 750 kcal and 1,800 mg sodium. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for hypertension patients due to very high sodium from multiple condiments, and poses a high glycemic load (GL ~52 per plate) for Type 2 Diabetes management. The red meat component raises cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis concerns. Portion control, sauce reduction, and vegetable augmentation are the primary mitigation strategies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in stir-fried kway teow with beef?
Is kway teow suitable for diabetics?
Sodium content of beef kway teow
Flat rice noodles beef stir fry nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database for stir-fried flat rice noodles (char kway teow), cross-referenced with the ai_generated GrabFood input (confidence 0.7). The beef substitution for pork is a minor adaptation with well-understood nutritional implications. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for flat rice noodles. No exact dish-specific laboratory analysis is available, hence medium confidence.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for stir-fried flat rice noodle dishes as of 2024. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g per 100 g, consistent with trace levels from cooking oil used in stir-frying; this is a plausible trace value and not confirmed zero. 3. The dish name in the input ('716D Stir-fried Kway Teow with Beef and') appears truncated; the canonical name has been normalised to 'Stir-fried Kway Teow with Beef'. 4. Halal status is listed as false because the dish contains beef from a Thai-style restaurant context where halal certification is not confirmed; consumers should verify with the vendor. 5. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on flat rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct measurement for this specific dish exists. 6. Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (61 × 85.05) / 100 = 51.9, rounded to 52.3 to account for minor sugar contribution from sauces. 7. Nutrient source URL corrected to https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/ (official HPB Foodo portal) from the input record which cited https://foodo.sg/ — the authoritative HPB Foodo database is hosted at the HPB domain.

No forbidden sources used.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.9), sodium_mg (400.0), saturated_fat_g (2.2), sugar_g (2.2) all present as numeric values, plausible and consistent with HPB Foodo reference for stir-fried noodle dishes.

Organisation field explicitly states 'University of Sydney GI Database'. Estimation basis fully documented. No forbidden sources used.

Covers: what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrient profile. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium-driven), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, GI/GL-driven, correct human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used, machine key 'type2_diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, saturated fat + sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Additional rheumatoid arthritis condition preserved from input.

All string values in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — consistency check passed. Comprehensive multilingual retrieval coverage achieved.
